scenario: Xander Insurer attaches a boundary event to a Camunda 8.8 call activity that invokes a claims sub-process. The team handles 9500 claims per day and is choosing between interrupting and non-interrupting variants while needing to know how each option affects the called process instance and its variables.
question: How does an interrupting boundary event on a call activity affect the called process instance and its variables in Camunda 8.8?

options.text: The interrupting boundary event suspends the called process instance for PT5M and propagates its current variables to the call activity scope when the suspension ends
The interrupting boundary event terminates the call activity and the created process instance, and the variables of the created process instance are not propagated to the parent
The interrupting boundary event terminates only the call activity but lets the created process instance keep running and propagates its variables on its own completion path
The interrupting boundary event marks the call activity as failed and keeps the created process instance running so its variables can be propagated to the parent on retry

a.text: Incorrect. The interrupting boundary event does not suspend the called process for a duration; it terminates the call activity and the child instance together.

b.text: Correct. When an interrupting boundary event on a call activity is triggered, the call activity and the created process instance are terminated, and the variables of the created process instance are not propagated to the parent.

c.text: Incorrect. Interrupting boundary events terminate the call activity and its child process instance together; they do not leave the child instance running.

d.text: Incorrect. There is no retry-with-running-child semantic for interrupting boundary events on call activities; the child instance is terminated and its variables are not propagated.

explanation: An interrupting boundary event on a call activity terminates both the call activity and the created process instance. Variables held in the created instance are not propagated back to the calling process scope.
